reasoning:
Based on the FRED statistical analysis, the recent trend of the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage average is slightly downward (6.21% as of Dec 18, 2025). The seasonal prior suggests a bounce is somewhat likely (0.46 base), and the 10-year Treasury yield rose slightly on Dec 19 (from 4.12% to 4.151%). A small increase in the 10-year Treasury often precedes a small increase in the mortgage rate, so for Dec 28, I estimate 0.52.

For the subsequent dates (Jan 20 and Mar 21, 2026), the broader economic consensus among several key forecasters (MBA, Compass, Realtor.com) expects the 30-year fixed mortgage rate to be around 6.3% to 6.4% in 2026. This implies structural pressure to climb back >6.21%. A few, like Fannie Mae, expect a year-end 5.9%, but MBA explicitly forecasts 6.4% for Q1 2026. Given the current 6.21% is slightly beneath the consensus expectation of mid-6% range for early 2026, the probability of being >6.21% increases with time as the rate tends toward the 6.3-6.4% consensus. I'm placing the Jan probability at 0.61 and Mar probability at 0.65 (increasing uncertainty as time expands, but also higher probability because the forecast average for 2026 is higher than the current value).
